opp.ais_why:
• Hunter Engineering has deferred any S2P conversation until after their IFS ERP implementation goes live post-summer, with no AI cues in current notes.
• Owner Michael McCarthy confirmed the prospect is heads-down on ERP enhancement and asked to be reconnected later, indicating no current AI mandate.
• Qualified stage but effectively dormant with the next touchpoint pushed several months out and zero discussion of agentic or autonomous capabilities.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler (Manager - Indirect Procurement), speaker_id=1, notes: 'Need AI to read and summarize contracts, extracting term, renewal date, pricing, and payment schedule for PO creation'; 'There is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice'; 'Challenge tracking and consolidating data from distributed indirect buyers across St. Louis headquarters and Mississippi factories'
No Salesforce fields (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c) populated with quantified metrics
metrics.narrative: Hunter Engineering articulated qualitative pain but provided no quantified business case. Whitney Chrisler stated on 23 February 2026 that her three-person indirect procurement team (one buyer for Capex/negotiations, one contract manager, herself managing contracts) plus distributed buyers in St. Louis HQ and Mississippi factories struggle to 'keep track of' and 'consolidate data' from indirect spend across locations, and that they need AI-driven tools to 'make our lives a little easier' post-IFS-ERP go-live. Specific pain points: no receipt process for services that matches PO to invoice ('there is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice,' Whitney, 23 Feb call), and the need for AI to read and summarize hundreds of maintenance agreements, subscriptions, and service contracts to extract term, renewal date, pricing, payment schedule at a glance for PO creation and approval. No quantified impact provided (no cost of manual contract review, no cycle-time reduction target, no error-rate baseline, no savings goal). The business problem is confirmed but not sized. Quantifying the pain (headcount hours spent on contract review, AP matching errors, subscription renewal misses, potential savings from automation) is the immediate next move to build a defendable business case.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler present as sole Hunter participant, speaker_id=1
Whitney's engagement: asked detailed questions about AI contract summarization, eProcurement, IFS integration; described team pain; requested follow-up in six months
No evidence of advocacy: no stakeholder introductions, no competitive intel shared, no coaching on decision process, no internal push
Salesforce Next_Step__c (last modified 5 June 2026): 'Get in touch with Whitney post summer once IFS implementation is live' — rep-side note, not a buyer-initiated action
Zero contact since 23 February 2026 (123 days ago), confirming passive/dormant engagement
champion.narrative: Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager) is a friendly, engaged contact but not yet a champion. On the 23 February 2026 discovery call she was open, responsive, and articulated her team's pain clearly, demonstrating genuine interest in solving the contract-management and invoice-matching problems. However, she provided no evidence of advocacy, competitive intelligence, or access to power. She did not introduce other stakeholders (IT, finance, or her manager), did not share Hunter's internal evaluation timeline or shortlist, did not coach Zycus on how to win or navigate the decision process, and explicitly described herself as early-stage exploratory ('tipping my toe in to learning more about some tools... see what Zycus and other companies can do for us'), confirming she is evaluating multiple vendors in parallel. She asked Zycus to 'check back in in six months' (around August 2026) after the IFS ERP go-live stabilizes, a passive stance that shows interest but no urgency or internal advocacy. Since that call (23 February 2026, 123 days ago), there has been zero follow-up engagement from either side — no additional calls, no emails, no stakeholder introductions. Whitney's engagement is now dormant. To develop her into a champion, Zycus must re-engage, deliver immediate value (answer her AI contract-summarization question, share a relevant customer story, offer a quick proof-of-concept on contract extraction), and ask her to introduce the Economic Buyer or IT lead. Current status: developing, but cooling due to 123-day silence. At risk of fading if not re-engaged soon.

sources: Salesforce Competitor field: Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Ironclad (source: SF Competitors__c, likely rep-entered, no buyer confirmation)

Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler: 'I'm tipping my toe in to learning more about some tools'; 'see what Zycus and other companies can do for us' — confirms multi-vendor evaluation but no competitors named
No competitive intelligence from call, tasks, Next_Step, or field history
competition.narrative: Hunter Engineering is running a multi-vendor evaluation. Seven competitors are listed in Salesforce Competitor field (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Ironclad), but the 23 February 2026 call provided no competitive intelligence — Whitney did not name other vendors being evaluated, share a shortlist, or describe where Zycus stands relative to others. She stated only that she is 'tipping my toe in to learning more about some tools' and wants to 'see what Zycus and other companies can do for us,' confirming parallel exploratory conversations with multiple vendors but no detail on who. The Salesforce competitor list appears to be a rep-entered assumption or CRM default (common P2P/S2P vendor set), not intelligence from the buyer. No other Avoma calls, tasks, or Next_Step references mention competitors by name. No indication of incumbent vendor being replaced (Hunter is implementing a NEW ERP, IFS, with no prior source-to-pay tooling mentioned). The competitive landscape is largely unknown: no shortlist, no preferred vendor, no bake-off timeline, no competitive differentiators tested. Zycus emphasized its AI/Merlin contract-summarization capabilities and IFS integration on the call, which aligns well with Whitney's stated pain, but whether competitors have matched or beaten that positioning is unknown. The wedge is Merlin AI for contract intelligence (Whitney's top stated need) plus proven IFS ERP integration. The activation move is to re-engage Whitney, directly ask who else she is evaluating and where Hunter is in the process, and deliver a targeted proof-of-concept (AI contract extraction demo on a sample Hunter maintenance agreement) to differentiate Zycus early. Current threat level: unknown but assumed high (seven named competitors, early stage, no Zycus differentiation established yet).

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler (Manager - Indirect Procurement, pain owner), speaker_id=1: 'We have hundreds of subscriptions, hundreds of maintenance agreements... Do you have AI that reads some of those things and fills in a list of information that I require?'; 'I need to understand at a glance without having to read multiple pages of contract'; 'There is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice'; 'Challenge tracking and consolidating data from distributed indirect buyers'

Avoma notes, 23 February 2026: 'Need AI to read and summarize contracts, extracting term, renewal date, pricing, and payment schedule for PO creation'; 'There is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice' (note_type: pain_point)
identify_pain.narrative: Hunter Engineering has clearly articulated operational pain in indirect procurement and contract management. Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager, pain owner) stated on 23 February 2026 that her three-person team manages hundreds of maintenance agreements, subscriptions, and service contracts manually, with no efficient way to extract key terms (contract duration, renewal dates, pricing, payment schedules) at a glance for PO creation and approval. Specific pain: 'We have hundreds of subscriptions, hundreds of maintenance agreements... Do you have AI that reads some of those things and fills in a list of information that I require in order to make some decisions?' and 'I need to understand [term, renewal date, pricing, payment schedule] at a glance without having to read multiple pages of contract.' Second pain point: no receipt/matching process for services invoices. Whitney stated, 'There is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice. And so how do you document that it's payable?' — confirming AP struggles to validate and pay service invoices without a clear PO-to-invoice match, creating manual workload and payment delays. Third pain: distributed buyers across St. Louis HQ and Mississippi factories make it difficult to 'keep track of' and 'consolidate data' on indirect spend. The pain is operational (manual contract review, AP bottlenecks, lack of visibility) and impacts Whitney's small team directly. No quantified impact yet (hours spent, error rate, late payments), but the pain is confirmed and owned by the manager responsible for solving it.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026: no discussion of contracting, legal, InfoSec, or implementation mechanics
No Salesforce field references to paper process or contract terms
paper_process.narrative: No paper process or contracting mechanics discussed. The 23 February 2026 call was a high-level discovery conversation focused on functionality (contract AI, eProcurement, invoice matching, IFS integration). No mention of MSA, SOW, legal review, procurement approval workflows, InfoSec review, or IT/ERP integration scoping. Whitney did not describe Hunter's contracting or implementation process. No current contract expiration date on file (Current_Contract_Expiration__c would be in Q2 query, but not referenced in the call or Next_Step). No indication whether Hunter uses a standard MSA, requires vendor InfoSec questionnaires, or has a lengthy legal review cycle. No SI/partner involvement mentioned. The paper process is completely unknown and will only surface once Hunter moves from exploration to formal evaluation (likely post-IFS stabilization, end of 2026 per Whitney). The next move is to ask Whitney about Hunter's typical contract and implementation process during re-engagement, and to map out expected timeline from vendor selection to go-live.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026: no budget, approval, or economic-buyer discussion
Salesforce OpportunityContactRole query returned zero contact roles (no EB, DM, or Executive Sponsor role assigned)

Account-level contact list includes Directors (Kurt Molitor - Facilities, Karlos Smith - IT, Lauren Crews - Business Technology Solutions, Sam Lackner - Accounting) and one VP (Mark Stirnemann - Research Development Engineering), but none confirmed as involved in THIS opportunity's scope (indirect procurement tooling)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager) is the sole engaged contact; she manages a three-person team and stated she 'takes care of most of the contracts' but gave no indication she controls budget or commercial authority for a $100k software purchase. No discussion of budget, approval process, or who signs off on procurement software spend occurred on the 23 February 2026 call. No VP/Director of Procurement, CFO, or C-level sponsor mentioned or engaged. The Salesforce field X10a_Sponsor__c and Who_will_approve_budget__c are not populated (Q3 query would clarify, but based on available Q1 data and the call, no EB signal). For a $100k+ source-to-pay platform purchase at a manufacturing company, the likely Economic Buyer is the VP of Operations, VP of Procurement, or CFO. Identifying and engaging the EB is a critical gate before any commercial or pricing conversation can proceed. Recommended move: ask Whitney directly who approves software purchases of this size and request an introduction to that person or their team as part of re-engagement.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler: 'I'm tipping my toe in'; 'I anticipate that I'm looking for some tools'; 'Not until end of the year' for serious evaluation
Only one call, one participant (Whitney), no multi-stakeholder engagement, no follow-on calls scheduled beyond the generic 'check back in six months'
Salesforce field What_is_the_decision_process__c not populated (Q3 query would clarify, but based on Q1 and call evidence, it is gap)
decision_process.narrative: No decision process mapped. The 23 February 2026 call was a single discovery/exploratory conversation with Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager), no other Hunter stakeholders present. Whitney provided no detail on how Hunter will evaluate vendors, who else must be involved, or what approval chain is required for a software purchase of this size. She stated she is in the 'tipping my toe' phase and plans to 'start looking at things' toward end of year (2026) after the IFS ERP stabilizes post-go-live, but did not describe a formal process (RFP, vendor shortlist, POC, committee review, etc.). No IT stakeholder (Karlos Smith, Director IT, or Lauren Crews, Director Business Technology Solutions) joined the call to discuss IFS integration or technical fit, and no finance stakeholder to discuss budget. No indication whether procurement software decisions are made by Whitney's team alone, require VP/C-level sign-off, or involve a cross-functional committee. The call sequence itself shows only one touchpoint with one person — a single-threaded, early-stage conversation with no escalation or broadening. Hunter's actual decision process is unknown. The next move is to re-engage Whitney and directly ask: who else needs to be involved in evaluating this, what does your typical software selection process look like, and when do you plan to make a decision.

sources: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler: 'I'm tipping my toe into learning more about some tools'; 'I'm looking to see what Zycus and other companies can do for us'; 'Need AI to read and summarize contracts'; 'Looking for tools that will enhance what IFS is giving us'
No Salesforce fields (Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c) populated with formal criteria
decision_criteria.narrative: Whitney articulated functional must-haves on the 23 February 2026 call, but no formal evaluation criteria, scoring rubric, or RFP requirements have been documented. Her stated needs: (1) AI-driven contract summarization to extract term, renewal date, pricing, payment schedule from hundreds of maintenance agreements and subscriptions at a glance; (2) services invoice matching (no current process to match service POs to invoices for AP approval); (3) eProcurement with preapproved vendor catalogs and punch-outs for faster requisitioning; (4) integration with the relevant stakeholder (their new ERP, go-live April/May 2026); (5) source-to-pay modules to 'enhance what IFS is giving us' and track distributed indirect buyers across St. Louis and Mississippi. Whitney emphasized she is 'tipping my toe in' and evaluating multiple tools ('see what Zycus and other companies can do for us'), confirming this is a multi-vendor evaluation with no decision criteria formalized yet. No indication of a formal RFP process, no timeline for vendor selection, no weighted scoring shared. Zycus has not yet run a deeper demo, use-case validation, or workshop to map their capabilities to Hunter's specific workflows. The next move is to convert Whitney's functional wish-list into a formal fit session or BRD review and understand how Hunter will score vendors (price, ease of integration with IFS, AI/Merlin capabilities, implementation timeline, references).

ai_fit_signal.summary: Hunter Engineering expressed clear AI appetite on the 23 February 2026 call. Whitney Chrisler's first and most-emphasized pain point was the need for AI to read and summarize hundreds of maintenance agreements and subscriptions, extracting key terms (contract duration, renewal dates, pricing, payment schedules) at a glance for PO creation and approval, eliminating the manual burden of reading 'multiple pages of contract.' She asked explicitly, 'Do you have AI that reads some of those things and fills in a list of information that I require in order to make some decisions?' — a direct AI use-case request. She also responded positively to Bailey's description of Merlin chatbot, AI-driven RFP creation, autonomous negotiation agents, and AP invoice scanning. No resistance or skepticism about AI expressed; instead, AI was framed as the desired solution to her team's operational pain. However, no formal AI requirements or ROI targets were documented (no AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c or quantified AI-driven savings goal), and Whitney did not describe AI maturity at Hunter (whether they already use AI tools elsewhere, whether IT has AI governance policies, etc.). The signal is strong but not yet formalized into a scored evaluation criterion. AIS score/status not populated in Salesforce, so cannot corroborate the call read. Tier: AI Curious (clear articulated need for AI contract intelligence, positive reception to Merlin narrative, but no enterprise-wide AI strategy or quantified AI ROI target yet).

items.change: Opportunity created in Qualified stage, $100k amount, 31 March 2027 close date, products iContract+eInvoicing+Merlin, seven competitors logged

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 26 March 2026 in Qualified stage following a single discovery call on 23 February 2026 (one month prior to opp creation, likely logged retroactively). No stage progression, no subsequent buyer engagement, no field changes since creation except Next_Step update on 5 June 2026 (a rep-side note acknowledging buyer's request to wait until post-IFS go-live). The IFS ERP go-live milestone (April/May 2026) has passed with no follow-up from Zycus or Hunter. Deal is dormant for 123 days. No movement.

items.name: Whitney Chrisler
items.risk: Cooling. 123-day silence risks loss of engagement. Whitney is exploratory (evaluating multiple vendors) and has not advocated internally for Zycus or introduced other stakeholders. If not re-engaged soon, relationship fades.
items.title: Manager - Indirect Procurement
items.sentiment: Positive but dormant. Engaged and articulated clear pain on 23 Feb 2026 call, but has not re-engaged since (123 days silent). Requested Zycus check back in six months post-IFS go-live.

items.name: Karlos Smith
items.risk: IFS ERP integration is a stated requirement, but IT has not validated Zycus technical fit or integration architecture. If IT is not brought in early, integration concerns can block the deal late-stage.

items.title: Director, Business Technology Solutions

items.detail: Hunter Engineering explicitly asked Zycus to 'check back in six months' after IFS ERP go-live (April/May 2026), pushing any serious evaluation to end of 2026 at earliest. The 31 March 2027 close date is a placeholder; true buying timeline is unknown and likely extends beyond that. Whitney stated she will 'start looking at things' toward end of year after IFS stabilizes, but provided no firm commitment or urgency. Risk: long, indefinite sales cycle with no forcing function or compelling event to drive decision. Deal can drift for months. Mitigating action: establish a nearer-term milestone (proof-of-concept, fit session, or ROI workshop) during re-engagement to create momentum before end-of-year evaluation window.

No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or executive sponsor identified or engaged. Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager) is sole contact and likely does not control budget for a $100k+ software purchase. Without EB/exec alignment, the deal cannot progress to commercial/pricing stage, and risk of budget denial or deprioritization is high. Mitigating action: map and engage the EB (likely VP Procurement or CFO) during re-engagement.

Hunter is implementing IFS ERP (go-live April/May 2026) and requires real-time bi-directional integration with Zycus source-to-pay modules. Bailey stated Zycus has '1,400 touchpoints' with IFS and can integrate 'in real time,' but no IT stakeholder from Hunter was present to validate technical fit, integration architecture, or implementation complexity. Risk: if IFS integration is more complex, time-consuming, or disruptive than presented, or if Hunter IT raises concerns late in the cycle, the deal stalls. Mitigating action: engage the relevant stakeholder lead (Karlos Smith or Lauren Crews) early to validate integration approach and align on technical requirements.

Zycus failed to deliver a committed follow-up. Bailey promised to clarify with presales whether Zycus contract-management includes AI-based contract summarization and report back to Whitney 'within the next 30 minutes, an hour' (23 Feb 2026). No evidence this follow-up occurred (overdue 123 days). This unmet commitment undermines credibility and trust, especially on the AI/Merlin capability that is Zycus's primary differentiation and Whitney's top pain point. Risk: Whitney may perceive Zycus as unreliable or question whether Zycus actually has the AI contract-summarization feature she needs. Mitigating action: acknowledge the overdue follow-up explicitly during re-engagement ('I owe you an answer from February'), deliver the clarification with proof (live Merlin demo or customer case study), and rebuild credibility.

Seven competitors in play (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Ironclad) with no competitive intelligence, no shortlist, and no indication of Zycus standing. Whitney is running parallel exploratory conversations with multiple vendors. Risk: Zycus is one of many and has not differentiated or built champion advocacy to navigate the evaluation. If a competitor establishes stronger executive relationships, delivers proof-of-concept first, or locks in a preferred-vendor position, Zycus loses. Mitigating action: deliver a differentiated proof-of-concept (Merlin AI contract-extraction demo) immediately upon re-engagement to establish early competitive separation.

champion_strength.source: Avoma call 23 February 2026; 123-day silence since last engagement
champion_strength.summary: Whitney Chrisler (Manager - Indirect Procurement) is a friendly contact who engaged positively on 23 February 2026, articulated clear pain, and demonstrated interest in Zycus capabilities. However, she is not yet a champion: she has provided no internal advocacy, no stakeholder introductions, no competitive intelligence, and no coaching on Hunter's decision process. She explicitly stated she is 'tipping my toe in' and evaluating multiple vendors, a passive exploratory stance. Since the call (123 days ago), she has not re-engaged, and Zycus has not followed up. Her engagement is now dormant and at risk of fading. To develop Whitney into a true champion, Zycus must re-engage with immediate value (AI contract-extraction proof-of-concept, customer ROI story), answer her overdue AI-summarization question, and ask her to introduce the Economic Buyer and IT stakeholders. Current strength: developing but cooling.

confidence_signals.summary: Deal is cooling. Only one buyer touchpoint on record (23 February 2026, 123 days ago). Whitney Chrisler engaged positively on that call but explicitly asked Zycus to 'check back in six months' post-IFS go-live, a passive stance indicating no near-term urgency. Since then, zero buyer engagement and zero Zycus follow-up (Bailey's promised within-one-hour clarification on AI contract-summarization never delivered, overdue 123 days). The IFS ERP go-live milestone (April/May 2026) passed approximately 12 weeks ago with no re-engagement from either side. Whitney's engagement is now dormant. No stakeholder introductions, no multi-threading, no champion advocacy. Single-threaded to a Manager-level contact with no EB/IT involvement. Seven competitors in play with no Zycus differentiation established. Deal trajectory: cooling toward lost unless re-engagement succeeds soon.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 26 March 2026, Stage=Qualified, 278 days to close (31 March 2027). Time in Qualified: approximately 92 days (created in Qualified stage per timestamp). Last buyer engagement: 23 February 2026 (123 days ago). No field history showing stage progression, no subsequent calls, and LastActivityDate predates opportunity creation (data anomaly), confirming zero post-creation buyer engagement. The IFS go-live forcing function (April/May 2026) came and went with no follow-up. Zero movement in 123 days forces Off Track.
evidence: Only one buyer call on record: 23 February 2026 (123 days ago), discovery/exploratory, Whitney Chrisler (Manager - Indirect Procurement) confirming IFS ERP go-live planned for 1 April 2026 (possibly 1 May), and asking Zycus to check back in six months post-go-live to revisit source-to-pay tooling.

Next_Step__c (last modified 5 June 2026) states 'Currently enhancing their existing ERP - IFS and confirmed that will revisit this opp post summer for S2P. Get in touch with Whitney post summer once IFS implementation is live.' This is a rep-side note, not buyer engagement; it acknowledges the buyer's request to wait.
LastActivityDate = 23 February 2026, predating the opportunity creation date (26 March 2026) by one month — data anomaly indicating no logged post-creation activity.
No field history showing stage moves, amount changes, or close-date slips. No subsequent tasks, emails, or calls logged since the single discovery call.

The IFS go-live milestone (April/May 2026) has now passed (~12 weeks ago as of 26 June 2026). The buyer's requested six-month check-in window would open around September/October 2026 (still 10-16 weeks out), but the deal has been silent for the entire 123-day period since initial contact.

Seven competitors named in Salesforce Competitor field (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Ironclad), but zero competitive intelligence gathered — no shortlist, no evaluation timeline, no preferred vendor signal. Whitney stated she is 'tipping my toe in to learning more about some tools,' indicating early exploratory phase with multiple vendors being evaluated in parallel.

Amount = $100k, but no pricing conversation, no ROI discussion, no multi-threading beyond Whitney (Manager - Indirect Procurement). No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or executive sponsor identified or engaged.

Stage = Qualified, but lacks the depth for Qualified: no confirmed pain quantified, no decision process mapped, no champion evidence (Whitney is a friendly contact, not yet a champion — she has provided no introductions, no advocacy, no competitive intel).
Forecast category = Pipeline, but the deal is dormant. Recommended forecast: Omitted (remove from active forecast until re-engagement succeeds).
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal remains frozen. Zero buyer contact for 123 days since 23 Feb discovery call. IFS go-live (April/May 2026) passed without re-engagement. Buyer explicitly deferred to post-summer; no follow-up attempted. Multi-competitor environment, no champion, complete radio silence.

best_practice_check.summary: Deal is cold (123 days silent) and single-threaded (only Whitney Chrisler engaged, no EB/IT/finance involved). Seven competitors in play with no Zycus differentiation established. The IFS go-live milestone (April/May 2026) has passed; Hunter asked Zycus to check back in six months (around August 2026), but no re-engagement plan is in place. Immediate actions: re-engage Whitney with value (AI contract-extraction proof-of-concept), map the Economic Buyer and IT lead, and establish a multi-threaded champion-building motion before the deal fades entirely.

competitive_position.summary: Hunter Engineering is evaluating multiple source-to-pay vendors in parallel; Zycus is one of several in early discovery. Seven competitors named in Salesforce (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Ironclad), but no buyer-confirmed shortlist, no competitive intelligence, and no indication of who is leading. Whitney Chrisler stated on 23 February 2026 that she is 'tipping my toe in' and wants to 'see what Zycus and other companies can do,' confirming exploratory phase with no vendor preference expressed. No competitive bake-off, no reference to pricing or capabilities comparisons. All seven competitors are marked active/unknown threat level due to lack of intelligence. Zycus differentiation attempt: emphasized AI/Merlin contract-summarization (aligns with Whitney's top pain) and IFS ERP integration (~1,400 touchpoints, real-time bi-directional). No feedback from Whitney on whether this resonated or how it compares to others. Current position: unknown, assumed middle-of-pack in a wide-open early-stage evaluation. Single strongest current threat: Coupa (listed first in SF Competitor field, likely due to brand recognition and common P2P shortlist placement, but no buyer evidence of Coupa engagement or preference).

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus emphasized AI/Merlin capabilities on the 23 February 2026 call, directly addressing Whitney Chrisler's top stated pain (AI contract summarization to extract term, renewal, pricing, payment schedule from hundreds of maintenance agreements and subscriptions at a glance). Bailey Erazo presented Merlin chatbot for eProcurement (requisition creation, PO approval via Microsoft Teams) and mentioned AI-driven RFP question generation, autonomous negotiation agents, and AP Smartdesk invoice scanning. Whitney responded positively to the AI narrative, asking specifically whether Zycus contract-management includes AI-based summarization. However, Bailey could not confirm the capability on the call ('I'm not 100% sure, let me ask presales and get back to you within 30 minutes to an hour') and never followed up (overdue 123 days). This unmet commitment undermines AI positioning credibility. Additionally, the Merlin/AI story was presented as a feature list, not a business-outcome narrative tied to Whitney's quantified pain (hours saved on contract review, faster PO approval, reduced AP errors). No proof-of-concept, no customer ROI story, no live demo of Merlin extracting terms from a sample Hunter contract. AI positioning is under-delivered: the narrative started strong but was not validated, differentiated, or tied to measurable outcomes. Score: partial. To strengthen, Zycus must deliver the overdue AI-summarization answer, provide a live Merlin proof-of-concept on a Hunter contract, and quantify the ROI (e.g., 'Merlin reduced contract-review time by 80% for, saving 15 hours/week for a three-person procurement team').

items.quote: Whitney Chrisler, 23 Feb 2026: 'We have hundreds of subscriptions, hundreds of maintenance agreements... Do you have AI that reads some of those things and fills in a list of information that I require in order to make some decisions? ... I need to understand at a glance without having to read multiple pages of contract.' Bailey (Zycus): 'I would imagine yes... let me ask presales on my team, I can get back to you on that.'
items.source: Avoma call 23 February 2026, Whitney Chrisler + Bailey Erazo
items.criterion: AI contract summarization (extract term, renewal date, pricing, payment schedule from maintenance agreements and subscriptions at a glance)

items.quote: Whitney, 23 Feb 2026: 'There is no receipt process for services that matches a purchase order with an invoice. And so how do you document that it's payable?' Bailey: 'The PO is essentially what would get scanned for the two 3 way matching to make sure that the good services received match the purchase requisition sent out.'
items.criterion: Services invoice matching (PO-to-invoice validation for services where no physical receipt exists)

items.quote: Whitney, 23 Feb 2026: 'Do you have preapproved vendors and punch outs that we can set up for all those? So the requisitioning can be done more expeditiously.' Bailey: 'Absolutely. Yep. You can prefer vendors, prefer punch outs for even items.'
items.criterion: eProcurement with preapproved vendor catalogs and punch-outs for faster requisitioning

items.quote: Bailey, 23 Feb 2026: 'We have about 1,400 different touch points between all of these different modules. We can integrate any data that needs to live within Zycus or within an ERP like IFS in real time. This would essentially be just like a bolt on to it, help you more with the procurement functions and then sending that information over into IFS.' Whitney: 'That's what I'm looking for.'
items.criterion: Integration with IFS ERP (real-time, bi-directional, minimal disruption to IFS workflows)

items.quote: Whitney, 23 Feb 2026: 'We have some distributed indirect buyers in our factories, and so I'm gonna want to keep track of what they're doing. Accumulating data on the buyers from our Mississippi operations in addition to our people here in Saint Louis.' Bailey presented Spend Analysis module: 'cutting, splicing data throughout the whole system, however you want, making spin cubes.'
items.criterion: Ability to track and consolidate indirect spend data from distributed buyers (St. Louis HQ + Mississippi factories)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Hunter Engineering's stated needs align well with Zycus iContract, eInvoicing, and Merlin AI capabilities, but fit has not been validated through a deeper demo, use-case session, or BRD review. Whitney Chrisler articulated clear functional requirements on 23 February 2026: AI contract summarization (extract term, renewal, pricing, payment schedule from hundreds of agreements), services invoice matching (no current PO-to-invoice process for services), eProcurement with preapproved vendor catalogs, and integration with IFS ERP. Zycus rep (Bailey Erazo) presented a high-level source-to-pay platform overview emphasizing contract AI, AP Smartdesk for invoice matching, eProcurement with catalogs/punch-outs, and IFS integration (~1,400 touchpoints). Whitney acknowledged fit potential ('that's what I'm looking for') but requested clarification on whether Zycus contract-management module includes AI-based summarization (Bailey could not confirm on the call and committed to follow up within one hour — no evidence that follow-up occurred). No deeper validation of fit: no demo of Merlin AI extracting terms from a sample Hunter contract, no IFS integration architecture review with Hunter IT, no discussion of implementation timeline or user adoption. Fit is assumed aligned on paper but unproven in practice. Risk: if Zycus cannot deliver AI contract-summarization at the depth Whitney needs, or if IFS integration is more complex than presented, fit weakens.

hard.ais_why:
• Hunter Engineering has deferred any S2P conversation until after their IFS ERP implementation goes live post-summer, with no AI cues in current notes.
• Owner Michael McCarthy confirmed the prospect is heads-down on ERP enhancement and asked to be reconnected later, indicating no current AI mandate.
• Qualified stage but effectively dormant with the next touchpoint pushed several months out and zero discussion of agentic or autonomous capabilities.
hard.next_step:
- Currently enhancing their existing ERP - IFS and confirmed that will revisit this opp post summer for S2P. 

- Get in touch with Whitney post summer once IFS implementation is live

value.flag: Single-threaded risk: only Whitney Chrisler (Manager - the deal owner's manager) engaged; no Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, IT stakeholder, or executive sponsor identified or involved. For a $100k+ software purchase, Whitney likely does not control budget or final approval. If Whitney leaves, changes priorities, or loses internal sponsorship, the deal dies. Mitigate by multi-threading: ask Whitney directly during re-engagement who approves software purchases of this size and who from IT must validate IFS integration, then request introductions to those stakeholders. Broaden the relationship before the deal advances further.
